## [06:08 - 09:13]

Whoop, whoop, this slide is, there we go. So in 2016, I had never heard of direct response marketing. I had never had a website. I didn't even know what copywriting was. When I heard the word copy, copywriting, I pictured that like weird symbol with the C and the circle around it that you see on the bottom of websites. So completely ignorant about this industry. At this time, I was a stay at home mom with my two boys and I also spent a few hours a week teaching yoga classes in a church basement. I ran a very small yoga studio inside of a church basement. Now, this yoga studio meant the world to me. It had always been my dream. Oh, there we go. It had always been my dream to open a studio and charge very, very low prices for these classes because I wanted to reach people in the community who can't normally afford to go to big, fancy studios. This was especially important to me because when I was growing up as a child, I was homeless for most of my childhood. I was raised by a single mom and to say that we struggled would be an understatement. Obviously, we didn't have access to a lot of those like awesome mind, body things that people do. And so during my teen and early 20 years, I had a lot of issues. And it was yoga in my early 20s, a free yoga class that I took with a volunteer that finally helped kind of ground me in my body and give me a sense of self and helped me kind of turn my life around. So I really wanted to do that for other people. However, in mid-2016, I found myself going through a very unexpected divorce after 11 years of marriage. Not only was I really devastated about losing my husband, losing my marriage, I was also terrified because I didn't know how I was going to support my kids. I was going to lose my house. And even worse, I knew I was going to have to shut down the studio in order to get a real job where I made real money so I could pay the bills. At this time, I also was confronted by this very intense and equally irrational fear that me and my kids were going to be homeless and out on the streets. Because from my childhood, I associated being a single mom with being homeless. So that was like my internal reality. ## [09:14 - 12:19]

And again, since I didn't know how much money people were making online, I just decided I was going to set like this huge income goal for myself. And my big dream goal was that with this product, I wanted to make $2,000 a month. Because at $2,000 a month, I could pay my mortgage, I could buy some groceries, and then most of my time I could just teach extra yoga classes on this side for supplemental income. So I got started with my big dream business of making $2,000 a month online. And the first thing I started doing was trying to figure out, you know, how to write copy, how to do this. A couple weeks in, I found myself then running into another wall of fear and being like, what am I doing? I don't know how to do any of this. I didn't know anybody in the industry. I didn't know of any of these amazing things like copy accelerator. I didn't even know what books to read. And I was so scared. I'm like, there's no way I can do this. But the problem was I was also being chased by this fear of losing everything. So I found myself between these two walls of fear, like afraid to do it, but afraid not to do it. And I was paralyzed. That night, I called a good friend of mine, a woman who's much older and much wiser than me. And I was telling her about my situation. And she said, Emily, she's like, you need to think about fear. There are two ways you can think about fear. The letters in the word fear can either stand for fear, everything and run. Or they can stand for face, everything and rise. And that hit me like a ton of bricks. And that night when I hung up the phone with her, I just couldn't stop thinking about fear. And I started thinking back on my life at this point, I was 35 years old, had never really accomplished much of anything, but I always wanted to. I always had these big dreams. And then I give up because I get scared. I write like 100 pages of a novel and then get scared and throw it away. Or I wanted to start a business once and the night before I was going to sign the lease on this building, I got scared and I pulled out. And I realized that I was always just letting fear make me quit before I even got close to accomplishing what I wanted to do. And I started thinking about fear and how we react to it and the mechanism of fight or flight. And I could see very clearly that fear always put me into flight mode and I would run. And I started thinking, okay, what if I could take this same emotion and train myself to have it put me into fight mode instead. And the more I thought about it, the more I realized that fear was really just this like identity box that was keeping me living in this box of what I thought was possible for myself. And it was keeping me away from all of the things that were not yet in the realm of reality for me yet, right? Like these things weren't quite real for me yet, but I was too scared to cross from that cross over and get to them.

## [12:19 - 15:20]

Fear was literally the emotional manifestation of my own limiting beliefs. That from that point forward, whenever I felt afraid of something, I was going to force myself to move toward that fear and not away from it. ## [24:29 - 27:30]

## [27:30 - 30:35]

and a man named Hamza Munir. And they both said to me, Emily, I remember what it was like to be a newbie and how hard it is. And we feel bad for you. ## [30:35 - 33:39]

you get an offer that takes off and then it just goes. That is so not true. Like offers like, like their main goal in life is to die on you over and over again. So that was that second year in business for me. And it worked like that workaholism and that fear and that adrenaline worked and that's what then kept it going. Then I run into year three. And I was starting to feel more comfortable at that point, which meant that unfortunately, then I lost my drug of choice, which was my fear, I was no longer feeling afraid. So I didn't know where to get my energy from anymore. And that's when I hit really heavy burnout and really heavy adrenal fatigue. I was so tired that anytime I would sit up or try to stand up, I would get so dizzy that I'd have to lay back down. So most days that year I actually worked flat on my back in my bed with my laptop on my belly. And during that year, so mad at myself because I saw these people in this industry who'd been grinding away for years and having success for years. And I was like, I must be like the laziest worst person on earth that like only two years in I've lost my fire. And I don't want to work anymore. And I like hate this. And I hate everything about it when it's like I created this thing and then I suddenly hated it. So I tried a lot of things to overcome this burnout, like I read a lot of books. And I tried to like to find my passion again and find like a different source of inspiration. And that didn't work. Then I tried like morning rituals, I thought, well, if I just have the right morning ritual to get me in the zone, then I'll be back at working back to work. And that didn't work. I actually, I personally hate morning rituals. For me, they just feel like procrastination. I'd rather get my work done and then do the self care stuff later. So none of those things worked. But I'll tell you what did. The thing that got me through that third year of burnout is that I had to get very strict around my discipline with my time. And the first thing I had to start forcing myself to do was to take breaks. I forced myself to stop working nights and weekends. And that was really hard for me to do actually, because even though I hated working, I actually felt worse when I wasn't working. Like I felt really lazy and really guilty when I wasn't working. So my stress levels would go up when I took time off, but I forced myself to do it. And then the other really strict rule I put in place around my workday was that I had to spend the first three to four hours of my day doing only my work. Not anything else that anyone needed from me. I couldn't answer a single email. I couldn't look at Skype. I just had to spend those first three to four hours doing my own work. Because what I realized was I was working all of these hours and not getting much done because I was constantly

## [33:39 - 36:44]

distracted with what other people were pulling me into. That was a really game changing thing for my productivity to start doing that. And what I've noticed is that if I even look at a single email in the morning, just one email, I literally lose my whole day. I can never get back into that mental zone that I need to be in to write or to do the hard work I need to do. So shutting that off completely and protecting that time fiercely was really important to me. That year, I finally got my balance back. And toward the end of the year, I started to get my energy back. And that brings me into 2019, which was actually the worst year, kind of started turning into one of the worst years in my business. And that is because I ran into the deadliest of all entrepreneurial sins, shiny object syndrome. So by this time, you know, I had energy back, I was feeling more inspired, and I wanted to do something new. Because let's face it, it is way more fun to start a new business or a new project than it is to run an existing business and an existing project. But what I found was that like every time I'd start working on something new, then back to life, my current business will just start dipping. Kind of like, you know, when like you've got kids and like, all you want to do is like relax and share a glass of wine with your friends. And you want your kid to just chill in the corner. But every time you like start turn your back on them, they start drawing on the wall with a crayon. Like that was like the relationship I was having with my business. And I realized like I had to get focused again, partly because this business felt like such a fluke such luck that I was like, I don't think I'm ever going to be able to recreate this again, let's be honest. So rather than trying new things and trying to make something else work, like I really need to get focused and keep the current thing that's already working going. So in order to do that, I spent a lot of time looking at, all right, what is it, though, that was driving me so hard away from my current business and making me feel like I needed to do more. And it came down to three things that I think are pretty common in this industry. First is I have this feeling like no matter what I'm doing or how much success I've had, it's never good enough. I know a lot of people can relate to this, like we hit a goal and like we should be happy when like, it's not good enough, like I need more. And I was really struggling with that. So I thought I needed to do more and more and more. The second thing is that in this industry, we are a culture of quick starts. And every time I would go to an event, the first thing people would ask me is like, what are you working on now? What are you about to launch? And I would feel embarrassed to be like, not working on anything new, like just the current business. Sorry, that's it. That's all I'm doing. I felt like a loser by giving that answer. Finally, the third thing

## [36:44 - 39:48]

I realized is that I was suffering from major informational overload, because I was attending a lot of masterminds and a lot of conferences. And every time I would come home from one, I would feel like, oh my God, there's all these things I should be doing like a YouTube channel or a supplement line or podcasts. And like, I'm behind, I need to do all these other things. And I realized like, this is actually like kind of a dysfunctional thing that we have in this industry. No other professional career has this. We're like, imagine an attorney where they finally reach like big success and they make partner in their firm. Like never are their friends and family or peers coming to them and being like, okay, cool, but like now what are you going to do? Like, are you going to go take night classes to become a doctor? Like, what else are you going to do now? Like, no, nobody else does this. It's only in this industry. And I think this has started to happen not only because of the personality types who all are, but also because there is this belief that all offers fail and all offers die. And in fact, when I first started, I was told to expect my offer to last about six to 12 months and then it would be done. And that is kind of the normal cycle for offers. So we get busy thinking that we need to create new things and new projects to have something in the pipeline for when the thing that's working fails. But when we do that, what we're essentially doing is we're betting on and we're creating our own failure because we take our eye away from what's working and we start working on our backup plan. And I know that part of the reason why my offer had worked so long is because I was just keeping my eye on that. I really, really believe that it's not offers that fatigue, it's offer owners that fatigue. It's when we take our attention off of our offers, that's when they start to dip and try to die on us. So when I realized that, then I put more rules in place for, as you can tell, I like to put rules in place for myself. I'm a Capricorn. I like to make promises to myself. So I put three rules in place for the end of 2019 and all through 2020. The first is I was going to start nothing new. I was going to get ruthless about cutting everything, seeing no to everything. And the only thing I would work on is whatever one thing was going to be at the time that would make my affiliates more money. The second thing I did is I decided to put conference blinders on. And when I go to conferences, I decided I would use them mostly for networking, but then I would be on the hunt for like one, just one, one big takeaway to come back and implement in my business. But it had to follow the two rules of A, what would exist within my current business structure, and B, what would make the most money for my affiliates. And then finally, I made the very conscious decision in 2019 that I was going to continue to keep my product line and also keep my team very lean. And a lot of people start out with an offer and then they grow it into these

## [39:48 - 42:03]

big companies, right, with lots and lots of employees. And for me, I do better when I can focus on one thing with as little noise as possible. So I decided to keep my team small, so there will be fewer people needing things from me, emailing me, distracting me from things. Yes, there are fewer people to delegate things to, but in keeping my business structure really focused and lean, I don't need to delegate as much. And that was really pivotable, pivotable, pivotal for me. And again, a hard thing, right, to see all these other companies scaling to like massive revenue and like a team of 100. Let me tell you, many of these companies are doing great, don't get me wrong. Many of them have very high revenue numbers and very low profits. Many of them are actually going under a lot of months in a row because they can't support the structure. So for me, I like lean and mean. And I'll tell you, this has been the most important thing I've done in my business, kind of staying lean and overcoming shiny object syndrome because at the end of 2019 or mid 2019, my product went from like really dying and hitting my worst months in sales, like I thought it was completely over, to then scaling literally tripling in volume, tripling in sales and revenue, even before COVID hit, triple the sales I had ever seen in the four years, doing more revenue than I ever thought possible with just one single info product. And I know it's just because of the focus that I brought into it. So that is how I worked with shiny object syndrome. I know a lot of people can relate to that. And I feel like if you are finding yourself struggling or not hitting quite the level of success that you want to get, I would urge you to get really ruthless and start cutting and go all in on one thing that you can really grow and scale and put all of your energy to. So that is a wrap for me for today. Guys, thank you so much for your time. I don't know if we have any time for questions or any of that, but I just want to thank you so much for being here and listening to my crazy journey. Thank you.
